fact_summary: The International System of Units uses seven base quantities and units, including metre for length, kilogram for mass, second for time, ampere for electric current, kelvin for thermodynamic temperature, mole for amount of substance and candela for luminous intensity. Derived units are algebraic combinations of base units. A physical equation must be dimensionally homogeneous, meaning that every additive term and both sides have the same dimensions, although dimensional agreement alone cannot prove that a formula is physically complete.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against NIOS Secondary Science and Technology Chapter 1; base units, derived units and the limited role of dimensional checking were kept distinct.

fact_summary: Speed is distance travelled per unit time and is a scalar, while velocity is displacement per unit time and includes direction. Average speed uses total distance over total time; average velocity uses net displacement over total time, so the two need not be equal. Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity and can arise from a change in speed, direction or both. The slope of a displacement-time graph gives velocity, and the slope of a velocity-time graph gives acceleration for the interval represented.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against NIOS Chapter 9, preserving the scalar-vector distinction and the correct graph-slope interpretations.

fact_summary: Friction acts along the contact surface and opposes relative motion or the tendency of relative motion. Static friction adjusts up to a limiting value, after which sliding begins; sliding friction is generally smaller than limiting static friction, while rolling friction is often smaller still. Friction is useful for walking, gripping and braking but also dissipates mechanical energy as heat. It can be reduced by lubrication, polishing, bearings or streamlining, and increased by roughening surfaces or using tread.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against the force and friction treatment in NIOS Chapter 10; friction types and practical controls were recorded without assuming friction is always harmful.

fact_summary: Newton's universal law states that two masses attract with a force proportional to the product of their masses and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between their centres. Near Earth's surface, weight is the gravitational force mg and may change when the local value of g changes, whereas mass measures inertia and does not depend on location. In ideal free fall near the surface, all objects have the same gravitational acceleration when air resistance is neglected.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against NIOS Chapter 11; mass, weight and free-fall acceleration were separated to prevent a common competitive-exam misconception.

fact_summary: Linear momentum is the vector product of mass and velocity. The impulse of a force equals the change in momentum, and for an isolated system the total momentum remains constant because internal action-reaction forces are equal and opposite. Momentum is conserved in both elastic and inelastic collisions, but kinetic energy is conserved only in an elastic collision. In a perfectly inelastic collision the bodies move together after impact, so some kinetic energy is transformed into other forms.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against NIOS Chapter 10; momentum conservation was kept separate from kinetic-energy conservation.

fact_summary: A simple machine changes the magnitude or direction of an applied force. Its mechanical advantage is load divided by effort, its velocity ratio compares the distance moved by effort with the distance moved by load, and efficiency is mechanical advantage divided by velocity ratio, conventionally expressed as a percentage. An ideal machine has no energy loss and 100 percent efficiency; a real machine has friction and other losses, so its efficiency is below 100 percent even when it provides a large mechanical advantage.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against the mechanics material in the official NIOS Senior Secondary Physics book and used only for the simple-machine portion of the composite topic.

fact_summary: Temperature indicates thermal state and determines the direction of spontaneous heat flow, whereas heat is energy transferred because of a temperature difference. Heat flows from a higher-temperature body to a lower-temperature body until thermal equilibrium is reached. The heat needed for a temperature change is Q=mcΔT, so it depends on mass, specific heat capacity and temperature change. During a phase change at constant pressure, supplied or released energy can change state without changing temperature and is described by latent heat.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against NIOS Chapter 14; heat, temperature, specific heat and latent heat were explicitly distinguished.

fact_summary: The laws of reflection state that the incident ray, reflected ray and normal lie in one plane and that the angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection. Refraction is the change in direction of light when its speed changes on entering another optical medium; Snell's law relates the sines of the angles to the refractive indices. A ray bends toward the normal on entering an optically denser medium and away from the normal on entering a rarer one, except when it travels along the normal.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against NIOS Chapter 15; normal incidence was retained as the important no-bending exception.

fact_summary: Concave mirrors and convex lenses can form real or virtual images depending on object position, while convex mirrors always give an erect, diminished virtual image for a real object. The human eye focuses light on the retina by changing lens curvature through accommodation. Myopia makes distant objects unclear and is corrected with a concave lens; hypermetropia makes near objects difficult and is corrected with a convex lens. Presbyopia is an age-related reduction in accommodation and may require bifocal or progressive correction.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against NIOS Chapter 15, including image behaviour and the distinct corrections for common vision defects.

fact_summary: Sound is a mechanical wave and therefore needs a material medium; it cannot propagate through a vacuum. Frequency determines pitch, amplitude is related to loudness and wave speed equals frequency multiplied by wavelength. An echo is a separately heard reflection when the delay is long enough, while repeated reflections can produce reverberation. Ultrasound has frequency above the normal human hearing range and is used in imaging, distance measurement, flaw detection and cleaning because it can be directed and reflected.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against NIOS Chapter 18; mechanical-wave requirements, perceptual properties and ultrasound applications were kept separate.

fact_summary: Electric current is the rate of flow of charge, I=Q/t, and potential difference is work done per unit charge, V=W/Q. Resistance is the opposition a component offers to current and, for a uniform conductor under fixed physical conditions, increases with length and resistivity and decreases with cross-sectional area. Resistors in series carry the same current and their resistances add; parallel branches share the same potential difference and the reciprocal equivalent resistance is the sum of reciprocal branch resistances.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against NIOS Chapter 16; definitions, material dependence and series-parallel rules were checked together.

fact_summary: Ohm's law states that potential difference across an ohmic conductor is proportional to current when temperature and other physical conditions remain constant, giving V=IR. Electrical power is the rate of energy conversion, P=VI, and for an ohmic resistor may also be written I²R or V²/R. Electrical energy is power multiplied by time; the commercial unit kilowatt-hour is energy, not power, and one kilowatt-hour equals 3.6 million joules. Joule heating is proportional to I²Rt.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against NIOS Chapter 16, retaining the fixed-condition limit of Ohm's law and the power-versus-energy distinction.

fact_summary: A magnet has north and south poles; like poles repel and unlike poles attract, and cutting a magnet produces smaller dipoles rather than an isolated pole. Electric current creates a magnetic field around a conductor, with direction given by the right-hand thumb rule. A current-carrying coil behaves like a magnet, and a solenoid produces a strong, nearly uniform internal field. Inserting a soft-iron core makes a useful electromagnet whose strength can be varied with current, turns and core properties.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against official NIOS Chapter 17, covering magnetic poles, current-produced fields, solenoids and electromagnets.

fact_summary: An electric motor converts electrical energy to mechanical energy because a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field experiences a force. Electromagnetic induction produces an emf when magnetic flux linked with a circuit changes, which underlies generator action. Domestic appliances are connected in parallel so each receives the supply voltage and can operate independently. A fuse or circuit breaker is placed in the live path to interrupt excessive current, while earthing provides a low-resistance safety path for leakage current.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against NIOS Chapter 17; motor action, induction and domestic protection were separated from ordinary circuit calculations.

fact_summary: A generator converts mechanical energy into electrical energy by electromagnetic induction. Rotating a coil in a magnetic field changes the magnetic flux through it and induces an emf; the induced current direction opposes the change that produces it, as expressed by Lenz's law. Alternating-current generators use slip rings to deliver a reversing output, whereas a split-ring commutator can rectify the coil output in a simple direct-current generator model. Generator operation is therefore an energy conversion, not a creation of energy.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against official NIOS Senior Secondary Physics Lesson 19 as an independent generator source for the composite topic.

fact_summary: Matter is made of particles that have spaces, move continuously and attract one another. Solids have fixed shape and volume, liquids have fixed volume but take their container's shape, and gases have neither fixed shape nor fixed volume and are highly compressible. Melting, freezing, vaporisation, condensation and sublimation are physical changes of state controlled by temperature and pressure. During a phase change, latent heat changes particle arrangement without necessarily raising temperature.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against NIOS Chapter 2; particle behaviour and state changes were retained without treating all changes of matter as chemical reactions.

fact_summary: An atom contains a compact nucleus of positively charged protons and neutral neutrons, surrounded by negatively charged electrons in permitted energy levels. Atomic number equals the number of protons and identifies the element; a neutral atom has the same number of electrons. Mass number is protons plus neutrons. Isotopes have the same atomic number but different neutron counts, while ions form when electrons are lost or gained without changing the nucleus or the element's atomic number.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against NIOS Chapter 5; atomic number, mass number, isotopes and ions were explicitly distinguished.

fact_summary: The modern periodic table arranges elements by increasing atomic number. Elements in a group have related outer-electron configurations and often similar chemical properties, while the period number corresponds to the number of occupied electron shells for main-group elements. Across a period, effective nuclear attraction generally increases and atomic size tends to decrease; down a group, additional shells make atomic size increase. Metallic character generally decreases across a period and increases down a group.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against NIOS Chapter 6, with periodic trends stated as general patterns rather than exception-free identities.

fact_summary: Chemical bonds form as atoms attain more stable electron arrangements. Ionic bonding involves electron transfer and electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions, while covalent bonding involves shared electron pairs. A chemical formula gives the elements and their simplest combining ratio or molecular composition; total positive and negative charge must balance in an ionic compound. Valency helps construct formulas, but subscripts are reduced to the simplest whole-number ratio and do not themselves carry charge signs.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against NIOS Chapter 7; ionic and covalent mechanisms and charge-balanced formula writing were kept distinct.

fact_summary: A balanced chemical equation conserves the number of atoms of each element. Combination reactions form fewer products, decomposition reactions split a compound, displacement reactions replace an element, and double-displacement reactions exchange ions. Oxidation and reduction occur together: oxidation may be described as gain of oxygen, loss of hydrogen or loss of electrons, while reduction is the corresponding loss of oxygen, gain of hydrogen or gain of electrons. The oxidising agent is reduced and the reducing agent is oxidised.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against NIOS Chapter 4; reaction classification and the reciprocal roles in redox were preserved.

fact_summary: Metals are generally lustrous, malleable, ductile, sonorous and good conductors of heat and electricity, whereas non-metals are generally poor conductors and brittle when solid. These are trends with important exceptions: mercury is a liquid metal, graphite conducts electricity and iodine is lustrous. Metals commonly lose electrons to form cations and their oxides are often basic, while non-metals commonly gain or share electrons and many non-metal oxides are acidic or neutral.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against NIOS Chapter 27; widely tested exceptions were included so general properties are not treated as universal.

fact_summary: The reactivity series orders metals by their tendency to lose electrons. A more reactive metal can displace a less reactive metal from its salt solution, while metals below hydrogen generally do not liberate hydrogen from dilute non-oxidising acids. Corrosion is gradual chemical deterioration through reaction with the environment; rusting of iron requires oxygen and moisture. Painting, oiling, galvanising, electroplating, alloying and sacrificial protection reduce corrosion by blocking reactants or making another material oxidise preferentially.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against NIOS Chapter 27, including displacement logic, rust conditions and distinct corrosion-control mechanisms.

fact_summary: Carbon forms many compounds because it is tetravalent and shows catenation, bonding to other carbon atoms in chains, branches and rings. Hydrocarbons contain only carbon and hydrogen. Alkanes are saturated with single carbon-carbon bonds, while alkenes and alkynes are unsaturated and contain double or triple bonds. Members of a homologous series share a functional group and general formula, have similar chemical properties, and successive members differ by a CH2 unit while physical properties change gradually.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against NIOS Chapter 28; tetravalency, catenation, saturation and homologous-series rules were treated separately.

fact_summary: Polymers are macromolecules formed from repeating monomer units; addition polymerisation joins unsaturated monomers without eliminating a small molecule, while condensation polymerisation commonly eliminates a small molecule as links form. Ethanol and ethanoic acid illustrate common functional groups, and esterification between an alcohol and carboxylic acid forms an ester and water. Soap molecules have water-attracting and oil-attracting parts that form micelles, while synthetic detergents can remain effective in hard water.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against NIOS Chapter 28 for carbon compounds, polymers, soaps and detergents; fuel facts are independently sourced in the next record.

fact_summary: A useful fuel should release substantial energy at a practical rate, be reasonably safe to store and transport, and produce limited harmful residue. Coal, petroleum and natural gas are finite fossil fuels formed over geological time; combustion releases energy but also produces carbon dioxide and, when incomplete or contaminated, pollutants such as carbon monoxide, particulates and sulfur oxides. Renewable sources such as solar, wind, hydropower and biomass are replenished on human timescales but still have location, intermittency or environmental trade-offs.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against NIOS Chapter 12 as the independent fuel and energy-source record for this composite topic.

source_title: Building Blocks of Life — Cell and Tissues
fact_summary: The cell is the structural and functional unit of life, and new cells arise from pre-existing cells. In multicellular organisms, cells with related structure and function form tissues, tissues form organs and organs cooperate in systems. Plant tissues include dividing meristematic tissue and differentiated permanent tissues; major animal tissue groups include epithelial, connective, muscular and nervous tissue. Their specialisation allows division of labour while the organism remains an integrated whole.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against NIOS Chapter 21 for cell theory, levels of organisation and plant and animal tissue classification.

fact_summary: Before cell division, DNA is replicated so genetic information can be distributed to daughter cells. Mitosis maintains chromosome number and produces genetically similar daughter cells for growth, repair and asexual reproduction. Meiosis consists of two divisions after one replication, halves the chromosome number in gametes and creates variation through chromosome assortment and recombination. Fertilisation restores the diploid chromosome number by joining haploid gametes, so meiosis and fertilisation together maintain a species' chromosome count.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against NIOS Chapter 25 as the independent cell-division source, separating mitosis, meiosis and fertilisation.

fact_summary: DNA carries hereditary information, genes are functional segments of DNA and chromosomes package DNA with proteins. Alternative forms of a gene are alleles; an organism may be homozygous or heterozygous for a locus. In Mendelian inheritance, dominance describes the heterozygote's phenotype and does not mean an allele is more common or biologically superior. Genotype is the allele combination, phenotype is the observable expression influenced by genotype and environment, and segregation separates paired alleles during gamete formation.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against NIOS Chapter 25; genotype, phenotype, allele, dominance and segregation were defined without value-laden interpretations.

source_title: Life Processes — I: Nutrition, Transportation, Respiration and Excretion
fact_summary: Photosynthesis uses light energy captured by chlorophyll to form carbohydrates from carbon dioxide and water, releasing oxygen; it stores energy in chemical bonds. Cellular respiration breaks down food to release usable energy. Aerobic respiration uses oxygen and yields much more energy with carbon dioxide and water as major products, while anaerobic pathways release less energy and form products such as lactic acid or ethanol and carbon dioxide. Plants both photosynthesise and respire, although photosynthesis requires suitable light.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against NIOS Chapter 22; energy storage versus release and the fact that plants respire were retained explicitly.

fact_summary: An ecosystem includes a community of organisms and the non-living environment with which they interact. Producers capture energy, consumers obtain it through feeding and decomposers return nutrients from dead material; energy flows through trophic levels and is not recycled in the same way as matter. Food webs connect multiple food chains and are more realistic than a single chain. Interactions include competition, predation, mutualism, commensalism and parasitism, and a change in one population can affect several others.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against NIOS Chapter 29; energy flow, nutrient cycling, food webs and major interaction types were kept distinct.
